title: The Big Picture: The Capesizes
summary:
Main
By Mark Nugent
With the Capesizes less exposed to the impact of the Ukraine-Russia conflict, we look at what is currently driving the Capes higher and what risks may be present. Following a 15.4% decline on the day following the onset of the war in Ukraine, Capesize 5TC spot rates have increased by 54.0% to $21,596 per day. Less affected by shipments in the Black Sea compared to the smaller size vessels, the region accounted for just 2.5% of Capesize demand in 2021.

children.title: Main
children.summary:
By Mark Nugent
With the Capesizes less exposed to the impact of the Ukraine-Russia conflict, we look at what is currently driving the Capes higher and what risks may be present. Following a 15.4% decline on the day following the onset of the war in Ukraine, Capesize 5TC spot rates have increased by 54.0% to $21,596 per day.

children.title: Coal shift begins
children.summary: Capesize coal trade has remained strong so far in 2022, with 61.2m tonnes loaded across January-February, increasing by 11.3% YoY. However, a decline in demand from China as domestic production is ramped up has slowed overall Cape employment from coal, declining by 9.2% YoY in February.

children.title: Iron ore trades mixed
children.summary: Out of Australia, iron ore volumes have been relatively strong so far in 2022. Across January-February of this year, 139.0m tonnes of iron ore loaded on Capes, increasing by 3.4% YoY.

children.title: Fresh lockdowns in China
children.summary: In the last week, a surge in Covid-19 cases in China has triggered a wave of lockdowns in several regions across the country that may disrupt port operations in the coming weeks. Although we have seen the seasonal unwind in Capesize congestion in China following the Lunar New Year, in the last week we have seen a slight uptick again, with this likely to compound in the coming days.
